variables.content: For methylammonium lead halide perovskites, replacing the carrier effective mass $m^{*}$ in Fr├Čhlich polaron expressions by the reduced exciton mass $m_{\mathrm{ex}}^{*}=\left(\frac{1}{m_{\mathrm{e}}^{*}}+\frac{1}{m_{\mathrm{h}}^{*}}\right)^{-1}$ (with $m_{\mathrm{e}}^{*}$ and $m_{\mathrm{h}}^{*}$ the electron and hole band masses) yields quantitatively accurate values of the electronŌĆōphonon coupling constant $\alpha$ and derived polaron parameters for free-carrier transport.

The Feynman polaron model, in which a charge carrier interacting with longitudinalŌĆæoptical phonons forms a polaron with an effective mass that increases with electronŌĆōphonon coupling strength, provides an appropriate theoretical framework for the layered leadŌĆæhalide perovskite thin films studied here so that an increase of carrier effective mass in RashbaŌĆæsplit bands translates into measurably stronger Fr├Čhlich (LO) phonon scattering and therefore a larger experimentally fitted ╬│LO parameter.
      Feynman polaron theory (R. P. Feynman, Phys. Rev. 1955) and relevant applications to leadŌĆæhalide perovskites cited in the paper

In a polar ionic lattice such as an organicŌĆōinorganic hybrid perovskite, denote by m* the bare band effective mass (the effective mass in the absence of electronŌĆōphonon coupling) and by m*_{pol} the effective mass of the polaron quasiparticle formed by carrier coupling to the lattice polarization; under standard Fr├Čhlich-type electronŌĆōphonon coupling appropriate to large polarons in such polar lattices, polaron formation increases the carrier effective mass so that m*_{pol} > m*, i.e., polaronic renormalization produces an effective-mass enhancement that must be incorporated when evaluating excitonic reduced masses and their magnetic-field response. [8]

The sequence consisting of (i) ab initio DFPT evaluation of long-wavelength phonons, Born charges, and dielectric response and computation of the long-wavelength Fr├Čhlich vertex and its energy-resolved squared coupling density d(g^{2})/d\\omega to obtain the effective single-mode parameters \\tilde{g}_{\\mathrm{LO}}^{2} and \\hbar\\tilde{\\omega}_{\\mathrm{LO}}, and (ii) a Feynman-polaron calculation using the ab initio bare mass m_{0} and the Fr├Čhlich parameter \\alpha computed from those inputs, reproduces the ARPES-measured hole effective mass m_{\\mathrm{exp}}=0.26\\pm0.02\\,m_e for orthorhombic CsPbBr$_3$ within experimental uncertainty; therefore this ab initio electronŌĆōphonon evaluation combined with a Feynman-polaron treatment is a viable predictive method to compute carrier effective masses in lead-halide perovskites when the Fr├Čhlich mechanism dominates carrier dressing.

The unitless Fr├Čhlich electronŌĆōphonon coupling constant $\\alpha_{\\mathrm{e-ph}}$ for a carrier species in a lead-halide perovskite can be estimated by selecting a single characteristic longitudinal-optical (LO) phonon angular frequency $\\omega_{\\mathrm{LO}}$ (for example, from the peak(s) in the calculated $\\operatorname{Im}[1/\\varepsilon(\\omega)]$ spectrum), together with the computed optical dielectric constant $\\varepsilon_{\\infty}$, static dielectric constant $\\varepsilon_{0}$, and the bare-carrier effective mass $m$, and inserting these into the canonical formula
      $\\displaystyle \\alpha_{\\mathrm{e-ph}}=\\frac{e^{2}}{\\hbar}\\frac{1}{4\\pi\\epsilon}\\Bigl(\\frac{1}{\\varepsilon_{\\infty}}-\\frac{1}{\\varepsilon_{0}}\\Bigr)\\sqrt{\\frac{m}{2\\hbar\\omega_{\\mathrm{LO}}}}$,
      where $e$ is the elementary charge, $\\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant, and $\\epsilon$ is the permittivity of free space; using a single (or a small set of) effective LO mode(s) together with DFT-derived dielectric constants and effective masses produces $\\alpha_{\\mathrm{e-ph}}$ values sufficiently accurate to classify the material into the weak/intermediate/strong Fr├Čhlich coupling regimes for subsequent polaron-model calculations.
      Miyata et al., fig. S12; table S2

Using published electronic and dielectric parameters for MAPI (band effective mass m* = 0.12 m_e where m_e is the free electron mass, static relative permittivity ╬Ą_s = 24.1, highŌĆæfrequency permittivity ╬Ą_Ōł× = 4.5, and a longitudinal optical phonon frequency Žē_LO = 2ŽĆ├Ś9 THz), substitution into the standard Fr├Čhlich coupling expression yields a coupling constant ╬▒ Ōēł 1.2; applying the Feynman variational polaron treatment to this intermediate coupling predicts a phononŌĆædressing increase in the effective carrier mass of order 25% above the bare band mass and an electron/hole polaron radius on the order of five perovskite unitŌĆæcell lengths, such that charge carriers modelled as polarons of this size will sample local electrostatic potential inhomogeneities on the mesoscale produced by dipole domains.

The computed Fr├Čhlich electronŌĆōphonon coupling strengths ($\\alpha_{\\mathrm{e-ph}}\\sim1$ŌĆō3) and the FeynmanŌĆō┼īsaka polaron mobilities indicate that room-temperature scattering by polar longitudinal-optical (LO) phonons with frequencies up to roughly $150\\ \\mathrm{cm^{-1}}$ŌĆöthe low-frequency PbŌĆōhalide modes implicated in polaron formationŌĆöprovides the principal intrinsic limitation on charge-carrier mobility in $CH_{3}NH_{3}PbBr_{3}$ and $CsPbBr_{3}$; polaron-model mobility estimates that include this polar optical-phonon scattering are about one order of magnitude lower than mobilities predicted by an acoustic-phonon-only deformation-potential scattering model, and thus polar optical phonon scattering is identified as the dominant intrinsic scattering mechanism setting the experimentally observed mobilities in these lead-halide perovskites.

For a charge carrier in a polar semiconductor, representing electron--phonon coupling by Fr├Čhlich large-polaron theory with coupling constant $\alpha=\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_\infty}-\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}\right)\left(\frac{e^2}{\hbar}\right)\left(\frac{m^*}{2\hbar\omega_{\mathrm{LO}}}\right)^{1/2}$, where $\varepsilon_\infty$ is the ion-clamped dielectric constant, $\varepsilon_0$ is the static dielectric constant including ionic response, $m^*$ is the carrier effective mass, and $\omega_{\mathrm{LO}}$ is a single dominant longitudinal-optical phonon frequency, yields an adequate mesosocopic description of polaron stabilization energy in hybrid halide perovskites for the purposes of energetic comparisons with exciton binding.

Using the longitudinal-optical (LO) phonon frequencies and dielectric constants of the methylammonium lead halide perovskites CH$_3$NH$_3$PbI$_3$, CH$_3$NH$_3$PbBr$_3$, and CH$_3$NH$_3$PbCl$_3$, computed the Fr├Čhlich electronŌĆōphonon coupling constant $\alpha$, the polaron effective mass $m_{\mathrm{p}}$, and the polaron radius $l_{\mathrm{p}}$ for each compound. The coupling constant $\alpha$ was evaluated from
      $$
      \alpha=\frac{1}{\varepsilon^{*}}\sqrt{\frac{R_{\mathrm{y}}}{c h \omega_{\mathrm{LO}}}}\sqrt{\frac{m^{*}}{m_{\mathrm{e}}}},
      $$
      with $1/\varepsilon^{*}=1/\varepsilon_{\infty}-1/\varepsilon_{\text{Static}}$ defining the ionic-screening parameter, $R_{\mathrm{y}}$ the Rydberg energy, $c$ the speed of light, $h$ Planck's constant, $\omega_{\mathrm{LO}}$ an effective LO phonon frequency expressed in cm$^{-1}$ units, $m_{\mathrm{e}}$ the free-electron mass, and $m^{*}=m_{\mathrm{ex}}^{*}$ the reduced exciton effective mass used as the carrier mass in these expressions. The effective LO frequency $\omega_{\mathrm{LO}}$ is a spectral-weighted average of the LO-branch frequencies $\{\omega_{\mathrm{LO},n}\}$, with weights given by the spectral weights of the corresponding LO modes. The polaron mass was estimated from the Feynman expansion
      $$
      m_{\mathrm{p}}=m^{*}\left(1+\frac{\alpha}{6}+\frac{\alpha^{2}}{40}+\ldots\right),
      $$
      and the polaron radius from
      $$
      l_{\mathrm{p}}=\sqrt{\frac{h}{2 c m_{\mathrm{ex}}^{*}\omega_{\mathrm{LO}}}}.
      $$
      Numerically: CH$_3$NH$_3$PbI$_3$: $m_{\mathrm{ex}}^{*}=0.104\,m_0$, $1/\varepsilon^{*}=0.17$, $\alpha=1.72$, $m_{\mathrm{p}}/m_{\mathrm{ex}}^{*}=1.36$, $l_{\mathrm{p}}=51\ \text{\AA}$; CH$_3$NH$_3$PbBr$_3$: $m_{\mathrm{ex}}^{*}=0.117\,m_0$, $1/\varepsilon^{*}=0.18$, $\alpha=1.69$, $m_{\mathrm{p}}/m_{\mathrm{ex}}^{*}=1.35$, $l_{\mathrm{p}}=43\ \text{\AA}$; CH$_3$NH$_3$PbCl$_3$: $m_{\mathrm{ex}}^{*}=0.20\,m_0$, $1/\varepsilon^{*}=0.22$, $\alpha=2.17$, $m_{\mathrm{p}}/m_{\mathrm{ex}}^{*}=1.48$, $l_{\mathrm{p}}=27\ \text{\AA}$, where $m_0$ is the free-electron mass. The reduced exciton effective masses $m_{\mathrm{ex}}^{*}$ used for CH$_3$NH$_3$PbI$_3$ and CH$_3$NH$_3$PbBr$_3$ were taken from magneto-optical experiments, and the reduced exciton effective mass used for CH$_3$NH$_3$PbCl$_3$ was taken from density-functional-theory band-structure calculations with spinŌĆōorbit coupling.

In the weakŌĆæcoupling expansion the polaron effective mass $m_{\\mathrm{pol}}^{*}$ is related to the bareŌĆæelectron effective mass $m^{*}$ by
      $$
      m_{\\mathrm{pol}}^{*}\\cong m^{*}\\left[1+\\frac{1}{6}\\alpha\\right],
      $$
      where $m^{*}=3m_{l}m_{t}/(2m_{l}+m_{t})$ is the averaged conduction effective mass computed from the longitudinal mass $m_{l}$ and transverse mass $m_{t}$. Substituting the evaluated coupling constants $\\alpha=0.33,\\,0.23,\\,0.15$ for PbS, PbSe, and PbTe at $77\\,$K gives the fractional mass enhancements:
      - PbS: $m_{\\mathrm{pol}}^{*}\\cong 1.05\\,m^{*}$ (about a 5% increase).
      - PbSe: $m_{\\mathrm{pol}}^{*}\\cong 1.04\\,m^{*}$ (about a 4% increase).
      - PbTe: $m_{\\mathrm{pol}}^{*}\\cong 1.03\\,m^{*}$ (about a 3% increase).
      Therefore, measured electron effective masses under typical conditions for these leadŌĆæsalt semiconductors are within a few percent of the bare band effective masses.
